segments.s435-g73: خوش‌آمد is a predicative adjective/participial phrase meaning “was pleasing/was welcome.” The noun phrase سخن شاه گشتاسپ را (speech of King Gushtasp) is marked with را as the definite/affected object; the whole segment reads literally “the speech of King Gushtasp was pleasing.”
segments.s435-g74: بفرمود is a past/honorific verb meaning “he said/ordered/commanded.” فرخنده is an epithet “auspicious/fortunate” describing جاماسپ; the object جاماسپ را is again marked with را. The implied subject (from context) issues the command or summons directed toward Jamasp.
segments.s435-g75: که introduces the content of the order (“that/for”), and بنویس is an imperative or jussive form “write.” یک نامه is the direct object “a letter.” نزدیک اوی uses نزدیک “near” plus the older enclitic pronoun اوی (“him/it”) to mean “near him” or “someone close to him.”
segments.s435-g76: یکی here means “a certain / someone.” سوی is a directional preposition “toward / to (someone).” گردنکش is an adjective “rebellious/headstrong,” and کینه‌جوی is another adjective “vengeful / revenge-seeking,” together describing the unspecified person who is the addressee of the letter.
segments.s435-g77: که here functions as a linking/exclamatory particle introducing the blessing or entreaty. یزدان is ‘God,’ and سپاس (with the vocative ای on the following word) expresses ‘praise/thanks.’ جهان‌پهلوان is vocative—‘O world‑champion’—addressing the hero directly.
segments.s435-g78: که begins a causal/explanatory clause here (“for / because”). ما is the subject “we.” از + تو is a causal phrase “because of you.” شادیم is the present/continuous-state verb “we are glad,” and و روشن‌روان adds another predicate describing their state—“and light‑souled / cheerful.”
segments.s435-g79: نبیره is a noun ‘scion/offspring.’ که is the relative particle ‘who/that.’ The comparative construction از جان گرامی‌تر است uses از as the poetic comparative marker ‘than’ (‘dearer than life’), گرامی‌تر is the comparative adjective ‘more dear,’ and است is the copula ‘is.’
segments.s435-g80: به دانش literally ‘in/with respect to knowledge’ uses به to mark the domain (‘in the realm of knowledge’). ز جاماسپ is the comparative ‘than Jamasp’ with the older preposition ز. نامی‌تر is the comparative ‘more famous,’ and است is the copula ‘is.’ The clause states he is more renowned in learning than Jamasp.
segments.s435-g41: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘at the same time/meanwhile’ (literary/poetic). نامه پاسخ is a noun phrase (نامه = ‘letter’, پاسخ = ‘reply’) serving as the direct object of the verb نوشت, which is a simple past verb ‘wrote’ (archaic/epic style word order: time/adverbial — object — verb).
segments.s435-g42: به here marks direction/place (‘into/to’ or ‘in’ in this context: ‘into a garden’). باغ بزرگی is a noun + size nominal modifier (بزرگی used adjectivally = ‘a large/great garden’). درختی is an indefinite noun ‘a tree’. بکشت is an archaic past form meaning ‘he planted’ (epic past with b- prefix). Word order: prepositional phrase (به باغ بزرگی) + object (درختی) + verb (بکشت).
segments.s435-g43: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase ‘he said thus/so’ (چنین = ‘thus’; گفت here is the past of گفتن ‘said’). کز is the conjunction ‘that/from which/because of’ introducing the following subordinate phrase. جور چرخ بلند is a genitive/compositional phrase: جور = ‘injustice/oppression’, چرخ = poetic term for heaven/wheel of fate, بلند = adjective ‘high/lofty’ following the noun (poetic word order).
segments.s435-g44: چو is the temporal/interrogative ‘when’. خواهد + رسیدن is a periphrastic future (‘will reach/arrive’) where خواهد functions as the auxiliary future. کسی is the indefinite noun ‘someone/anyone’; را is the definite-object/topic marker attaching to کسی to mark the affected person; گزند is the noun ‘harm/damage’ serving as the semantic thing that ‘reaches’ (the construction literally: ‘will the harm reach someone’). Word order reflects older/poetic Persian where the verb (رسیدن) precedes or accompanies its affected object phrase.
segments.s435-g45: به here is adverbial (‘by/with’) indicating the means (‘by means of restraint’). پرهیز is the means noun ‘forbearance/caution/restraint’. چون in this position is interrogative/adverbial ‘how’. بازدارد is a present/subjunctive/epic-form verb meaning ‘restrains/keeps back’ (transitive). کسی is the indefinite object ‘anyone/someone’. The phrase thus asks rhetorically ‘by what restraint can anyone keep him back?’
segments.s435-g46: وگر is a conditional particle ‘if / but if’. سوی + دانش is a directional phrase ‘toward knowledge’ (سوی = ‘toward’, دانش = ‘knowledge/learning’). گراید is a present/subjunctive/epic-form verb ‘turns/leans’ (i.e. ‘if he turns/leans’). بسی is an adverb meaning ‘enough/sufficiently’ or ‘much’ and modifies the clause ('that will suffice' or 'to a great extent'). The clause gives the conditional: turning to knowledge suffices.
segments.s435-g47: پشوتن is the proper name (subject). بگفت is the past verb ‘said’ (epic past form). آنچ is a fused relative ‘that which/whatever’. درخواستی is the noun (or verbal noun) ‘(you) requested’ — together آنچ درخواستی = ‘that which you asked/requested’ (direct object of what was said).
segments.s435-g48: دل من is a possessive construction ‘the heart of me’ = ‘my heart’ (Persian places the possessed noun before the possessor). به here is instrumental/adverbial ‘with/by’ (i.e. ‘with goodness’). خوبی is the noun ‘goodness/kindness’. بیاراستی is an archaic/poetic past verbal form meaning ‘you adorned / you made (it) beautiful/fit’ (second-person past) — together دل من به خوبی بیاراستی = ‘you have adorned my heart with goodness’.
segments.s435-g113: همی + verb is a classical/perfective-progressive marker meaning 'continually / kept on (doing)'. آزمودش is a past-tense verb (آزمود = 'tested') with the enclitic object -ش = 'him/it'. به یک چندگاه is an adverbial phrase: به (preposition) + یک چندگاه 'for a short while / for some time'.
segments.s435-g114: This segment lists places where the testing occurred. The preposition به is repeated before each noun (a common poetic device) and functions as 'at/in/on' or 'in the setting of'. بزم 'banquet/feast', رزم 'battle/combat', نخجیرگاه 'hunting-ground' are simple nouns coordinated by و 'and'.
segments.s435-g115: به میدان چوگان is 'to/at the polo-field' where میدان 'field/arena' is modified by چوگان (the game 'polo'). The following و بزم و شکار continues the enumeration (و = 'and'). شکار here means 'the hunt' (the activity).
segments.s435-g116: گوی بود is an impersonal/classical idiom: گوی here is the verbal/impersonal 'it is said / was said' (not the literal 'sphere'), followed by بود 'was'. مانند + name introduces a simile: 'was said to be like Esfandiar.' In other words, the sentence reports a reputation: 'he was (said to be) like Esfandiar.'
segments.s435-g117: ازو is the contracted form of از او 'from/of him / because of him'; هیچ is the negative adverb 'at all/none' placed before the verb; گشتاسپ is the proper name (Gushtasp). نشکیفتی is the past form with negative prefix ن- + شکفتی (from شکفتن 'to blossom / to be pleased'), so together 'did not please / did not blossom' — i.e. 'Gushtasp was not at all pleased (by him)'. Word order places the oblique phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.s435-g118: به + verbal noun here marks means or instrument: به می‌خوردن 'by making (him) eat/drink'. می‌خوردن is the infinitival/gerund-like form 'to eat/drink'; اندر + -ش (اندرش) = 'in/at him' with the enclitic -ش = 'his/him' attached; بفریفتی is a past/narrative form of فریفتن 'to deceive' (classical b‑ prefix used in narrative forms), meaning 'you deceived (him)'.
segments.s435-g119: همی گفت is the recurrent imperfective narrative 'he kept saying / he used to say'. کاینم is an archaic contraction of که اینم 'that this', introducing a reported clause. جهاندار is a classical epithet 'the world-ruler / sovereign'. داد here functions as the verb 'gave' (classical diction) in the reported clause: 'that the sovereign had given this.'
segments.s435-g120: غمی بودم is 'I was (in) grief' — غمی (a sorrow) + بودم (first-person past of بودن). از بهر is a classical prepositional compound meaning 'for the sake of / because of'. تیمار داد here again uses داد as 'gave' and تیمار 'care/solicitude' — literally '(he) gave care' or 'given for the sake of care'; the clause expresses that the speaker was afflicted with sorrow on account of (the) care.
segments.s435-g105: The clause begins with the dative clitic بدو ‘to him’ followed by گفت (here the verb 'said') and the speaker name اسفندیاری (Esfandiyār): ‘Esfandiyār said to him’. The short phrase تو بس that follows is vocative + interjection: تو ‘you’ (addressing the addressee) and بس ‘enough/stop’ functioning as an imperative-like interjection.
segments.s435-g106: نمانی is from ماندن and here functions as a negative/future-like statement 'will not remain' or impersonal 'there will remain' in literary Persian. The locative phrase به گیتی means 'in the world'; جز او را is an exception construction 'except him' (او را uses را after the excluded item), and به کس means 'to anyone' or more simply 'any person' — together: 'there will remain in the world no one except him.' Note the archaic/poetic ordering (verb before its qualifiers and را placed after the excluded pronoun).
segments.s435-g107: ورا (to him) is the object clitic marking whom he found; یافت is the past tense 'found'; روشن‌دل is a compound adjective 'bright‑hearted/clear‑minded'; و links to the next adjective یادگیر, which here means 'quick to learn' or 'one who takes/learns' — together: 'he found him bright‑hearted and quick to learn.'
segments.s435-g108: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'from that time/after that'; همی is a frequentive/adverb meaning 'continually/from then on'; خواندش is the past verb with the object clitic ش — here خواندش means 'he called him' (gave him a name or called him so); اردشیر (the subject) follows in typical poetic word order.
segments.s435-g109: گوی بود is an impersonal/idiomatic expression 'it was said' or 'people said' (گوی = 'they say/it is said', بود = 'was'). با زور = 'with strength' (indicating physical power). گیرنده دست is a nominal phrase where گیرنده is a participial/agentive form 'seizing/grasping' modifying دست 'hand' — i.e. 'a hand that seizes' or 'a gripping hand'.
segments.s435-g110: A string of attributes describing the same person: خردمند (wise), و دانا (and learned), و یزدان‌پرست (and God‑worshipping/pious). The coordinating و simply links the adjectives.
segments.s435-g111: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; بر پای بودی literally 'was on (his) foot(s)' = 'stood/was standing' (بودی is past tense 'was'); سرانگشت اوی is 'his fingertip(s)' (اوی as possessive 'his'). The clause means 'when he stood upright, his fingertips ...' (poetic word order places the verb before the possessed noun).
segments.s435-g112: ز زانو 'from/above the knee' (ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'), فزونتر is the comparative 'more/greater/exceeding', بدی in this position is a poetic/preserved verbal form (here functioning as 'was' / 'appeared' — not represented in the provided senses), and مشت اوی is 'his fist'. Altogether: 'his fist exceeded/was larger than the knee.'
segments.s435-g89: This is a relative/partitive phrase: ز چیزی که بودش = “of something that was” with the location appended (به گنج اندرون = “in/within the treasure”). Note the enclitic -ش in بودش (بود + ش) = “it was” referring back to the thing; word order places the relative clause before the locative.
segments.s435-g90: This lists two items taken from that treasure: ز خفتان = “from the (bed-)clothes / bedding” (here a noun derived from the root for sleep) and وز خنجر آبگون = “and from a dagger (خنجر) that is آبگون (bluish/tinted). وز here functions as the poetic linking 'and/from'.
segments.s435-g91: Another list-phrase introduced by the partitive preposition ز “from.” برگستوان here is a nominal item (an armour/coat) rather than the verb sense; the string ز ... و ز ... enumerates several object-types (تیر و کمان = “arrow(s) and bow(s)”).
segments.s435-g92: Continues the enumeration: ز گوپال = “from Gopala” (a proper name or ethnonym here) and ز خنجر هندوان = “from the daggers of the Hindus/Indians.” The two ز phrases are parallel; هندوان is the genitive/attributive plural meaning “of the Indians.”
segments.s435-g93: Another run of partitive phrases: ز کافور = “from camphor,” وز مشک = “and from musk,” وز عود‌تر = “and from agarwood/oud.” The repeated ز/وز marks successive items; عود‌تر is the aromatic/wood sense of عود (agarwood).
segments.s435-g94: هم از عنبر = “also from amber(gris),” followed by a further list joined by و: گوهر و سیم و زر = “gems and silver and gold.” هم here adds the next group (‘also’), and از is the plain preposition “from.”
segments.s435-g95: ز بالا = “from the high/upper (i.e. the high-born or high in rank)” where بالا functions as a substantive adjective; و از جامه نابرید = “and from garments that were not cut” (نابرید = uncut/intact), i.e. seamless robes. The parallel ز/از construction again gives a list of sources/items.
segments.s435-g96: پرستار = “nurse/caretaker” (an item/person in the enumeration); وز here links to the next item (and/from); کودکان نارسید = “infants not yet ripe/fully grown” — نارسید used adjectivally to indicate immaturity (hence 'unweaned' or 'premature').
segments.s435-g65: ز introduces a source or cause (poetic 'from/through'); دانش پدر is a genitive sequence 'the knowledge of (his) father'. هرچ جست is an archaic perfect/past-stem used like a past or relative clause 'whatever (he) sought'; جست here is from جستن 'to seek'. اندر اوی is a prepositional phrase 'in him' (اندر = in; اوی = him/her/in him), linking what was sought to its locus.
segments.s435-g66: به جای آمد is an idiomatic clause 'came to its place / was realized' (به = into/in, جای = place, آمد = came/happened). و here connects the two result clauses ('and / then'). گشت is the intransitive past-stem used as 'became' (turned into a state) and با آب‌روی is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with a fair/handsome face/appearance' (با = with; آب‌روی = appearance/face).
segments.s435-g67: به + place phrase marks location here ('in a/an'): به بیگانه شهری = 'in a foreign/alien city' with بیگانه qualifying شهری. فراوان بماند uses فراوان adverbially 'for a long time / abundantly' and بماند (subjunctive/ present/future sense of ماندن) 'will remain/is left' — the verb presents duration/result.
segments.s435-g68: کسی ... نخواند is a negative/universal sense 'no one will/does ...' (کسی here used in the 'no one' reading because of the negative verb). نامه تو is 'your letter' (noun + pronoun). بروبر is an adverb/adverbial phrase 'in person / face-to-face / directly' modifying نخواند; نخواند is the negative third-person form of خواندن used here as future/negative 'will not read' or 'does not read'.
segments.s435-g69: به بهمن indicates the indirect object 'to Bahman' (به = to/for; بهمن is a personal name). یکی نامه is an indefinite phrase 'one/a letter' (یکی = one/a certain; نامه = letter). باید is the modal 'must/ought', and نوشت here is the bare past-stem used as an infinitive 'to write' after باید (classical construction: باید + past-stem = 'must write').
segments.s435-g70: بسان is a comparative 'like/as'. درختی is the indefinite/noun form 'a tree' that follows the simile. به باغ بهشت is a locative/genitival phrase 'in the garden of Paradise' (به = in/to; باغ = garden; بهشت = Paradise), completing the simile 'like a tree in Paradise's garden'.
segments.s435-g71: که here introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. داری is the present tense 'you have/possess' (دارى). به گیتی = 'in the world' (به = in; گیتی = world). جز او یادگار is a negative/exclusive phrase 'a memento except him' (جز = except; او = him; یادگار = memento), so the clause says you possess no other memorial in the world besides him.
segments.s435-g72: گسارنده is a participial adjective 'that relieves/dissolves/breaks' modifying یادگار (so 'a memento that relieves ...'). درد اسفندیار is a genitive 'Esfandiyar's pain' (درد = pain/affliction; اسفندیار = the proper name). The phrase as a whole appositionally describes that 'memento' as one that eases Esfandiyar's suffering.
segments.s435-g81: 'به بخت تو' is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'by/through your fortune' (به = by), followed by the verb آموخت used in the sense 'taught' (past). The direct objects فرهنگ و رای follow the verb; English word order is adjusted but Persian here places the instrument/agential phrase before the verb and the learned items after it.
segments.s435-g82: سزد‌گر is an archaic conditional/ concessive particle roughly 'if it be fitting' (literally 'one who deems fit'); it sets a condition for the following verb. فرستی is a jussive/imperative second-person form 'send' (you should send), کنون 'now', باز 'back/again', جای 'place/post'. The clause therefore gives a conditional command: 'if it is fitting, now send back a place.'
segments.s435-g83: یکی سوی بهمن literally 'one toward Bahman' — 'یکی' introduces a single messenger or one message to be sent, 'سوی' = toward/to, and بهمن is the addressee (the proper name Bahman). The following که introduces the subordinate time clause ('which/when in that time').
segments.s435-g84: چو + verb marks a temporal clause: 'چو نامه بخوانی' = 'when you read the letter' (بخوانی is the present/subjunctive reading 'you read'). به زابل is the locative 'in/at Zabul'. ممان is a prohibitive/negative sense here ('do not remain/stay'), so the whole second half advises 'do not remain in Zabul when you read the letter.'
segments.s435-g85: که introduces an explanatory clause: 'ما را' marks 'us' with the definite-object/experiencer marker را (here 'we' as the affected party). 'به دیدارت آمد نیاز' is an idiomatic expression meaning 'a need/urge to see you has come upon us' (آمد as 'came' + نیاز 'need'), so the whole means 'for we are in need of meeting you.'
segments.s435-g86: برآرای is an imperative meaning 'prepare/arrange' (order/organize); کار و درنگی is the object 'the affair and any delay' (درنگی = pause/delay) joined by و. مساز is a prohibitive imperative 'do not make' — together: 'arrange the matter and do not cause delay.'
segments.s435-g87: به رستم marks the indirect object 'to/for Rostam'. چو introduces the temporal clause 'when', and برخواند is a past tense verb 'read aloud/recited' (here the scribe performed the reading). نامه دبیر is the direct object 'the scribe's letter' or 'the letter read by the scribe.'
segments.s435-g88: بدان is a short adverbial meaning 'thereupon/at that' (ب functioning as a prefixed particle), followed by شاد شد 'became glad' (past). مرد دانش‌پذیر is the subject phrase 'the man receptive to knowledge' (دانش‌پذیر as an epithet meaning teachable/knowledge‑accepting). The clause thus states that he rejoiced upon hearing it.
segments.s435-g97: کمرهای زرین و زرین ستام is a noun phrase listing precious items. کمرهای (kamar-hā-ye) is plural definite/indefinite 'belts/girdles' placed before its adjectives; زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying کمرهای. The sequence 'و زرین ستام' links a second coordinated item: و = 'and', followed by زرین ('golden') plus ستام, here used as a descriptive past/participle-like word ('praised' / 'glorious') forming a compound nominal description ('a golden, praised ornament'). Word order is noun(s) → adjectives/epithets; ستام behaves like an adjectival participle modifying the implied object.
segments.s435-g98: ز یاقوت با زنگ زرین دو جام is another coordinated noun phrase giving two cups. ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing a material or gemstone association ('of ruby'), یاقوت = 'ruby'. با = 'with' links an instrumental or ornamental detail, followed by زنگ زرین 'golden ring/metal mount' (زنگ as a metal ring/ornament + زرین 'golden'). دو جام is the counted noun 'two goblets/cups' placed after the modifiers (typical Persian order: modifiers/agents before the counted noun).
segments.s435-g99: همه پاک رستم به بهمن سپرد places the direct object phrase 'همه پاک' ('all, wholly/purely') before the subject and verb for emphasis. رستم is the subject, به بهمن marks the indirect object/recipient with به + proper name ('to Bahman'), and سپرد is the past tense verb 'entrusted/handed over'. The order (object — subject — recipient — verb) is a poetic, topical ordering common in older Persian.
segments.s435-g100: برنده به گنجور او بر شمرد is a short clause describing what the recipient did. برنده functions as an agent noun 'the taker/victor/one who received/collected' (here 'the taker'), به گنجور = 'into (his) treasury' uses به to indicate placement/direction, او is the pronoun 'he' (subject), and بر + شمرد together form برشمرد 'counted up' (بر here is the verbal prefix meaning 'over/through' attached to the verb شمرد 'to count').
segments.s435-g101: تهمتن بیامد دو منزل به راه is a temporal/spatial clause: تهمتن (a proper epithet/name) is the subject, بیامد is the simple past 'came' (here 'arrived' or 'advanced'), and دو منزل به راه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'two stages on the road' — دو منزل (numeral + measure 'two stages') followed by the locative/goal به راه ('along the way' / 'on the road').
segments.s435-g102: پس او را فرستاد نزدیک شاه gives the subsequent action: پس 'then', او را 'him' (personal pronoun with the object marker را — here marking the person being sent), فرستاد is the past 'sent', and نزدیک شاه is a locative phrase 'near the king' meaning 'to the king's presence'. The object marker را confirms او is the direct object of فرستاد.
segments.s435-g103: چو گشتاسپ روی نبیره بدید is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو 'when'. گشتاسپ is the subject, روی here means 'face/appearance' and is the direct object of بدید 'saw'; نبیره ('scion/offspring') specifies whose face it is (the youth/scion). Word order is typical: temporal conjunction + subject + object phrase + verb.
segments.s435-g104: شد از آب‌دیده رخش ناپدید combines the light verb شد 'became' / 'happened' with the predicate 'ناپدید' ('vanished'). از introduces the source/cause phrase آب‌دیده, here idiomatically 'with tearful/ watery eyes' (literally 'water‑seen'), modifying the perception; رخش 'his steed' is the thing that vanished. The result clause reads literally 'it became — from tearful sight — his steed vanished', i.e. 'his steed vanished from his tearful sight.'
segments.s435-g33: پشوتن (proper name) is the subject; بیامد is the simple past verb 'came'. گوایی is a noun meaning 'eloquence' (here describing the manner or content of speaking) and is placed after the verb phrase in a poetic word order. بداد appears immediately after گوایی and would normally be a verb 'gave' (i.e. 'gave eloquence' / 'spoke eloquently'), but the provided sense list does not include that verbal sense, so the combination is best read as a single verbal idea 'came and spoke eloquently'.
segments.s435-g34: سخنهای رستم is a genitive phrase 'Rostam's words/speeches' (سخنهای = plural of سخن). همه is an adverb/quantifier 'all' (the whole). کرد یاد is an inverted poetic order of the common compound/idiom یاد کردن 'to remember' (کرد = 'did/made', یاد = 'remembrance'), so together they mean 'remembered' or 'recalled'.
segments.s435-g35: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same', linking back to what was just mentioned; زاری و پند و اروند is a coordinated list 'lamentation and advice and Arvand'. The final او is a pronoun attaching to the list (i.e. 'his ...'). Note that اروند here is a lexical item whose precise meaning is ambiguous in this line (could be a name or an epithet), so its exact sense is uncertain.
segments.s435-g36: سخن گفتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to speak' or 'speaking' and with از introduces the object/topic of speech: 'to speak about' (سخن گفتن از). مرز و پیوند او are the objects 'his border and (his) bond', where او is a possessive pronoun referring back to the person mentioned; the coordinate مرز و پیوند are governed by از ('about').
segments.s435-g37: ازان is a prepositional pointer 'from that/thereupon' (linking to the preceding event), followed by نامور شاه as the noun phrase 'the famed king'. خشنود گشت is a predicative verbal phrase 'became pleased' (خشنود = pleased; گشت = became). This is a standard past narrative construction in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s435-g38: گراینده را places گراینده ('the one who directs/urges') as the topical/definite item with the object marker را. آمدن is the verbal noun 'the coming/arrival', and سود گشت is a predicative phrase 'became benefit' i.e. 'proved advantageous' (سود = benefit; گشت = became). The whole line means that the arrival of that person turned out to be profitable/useful.
segments.s435-g39: ز رستم is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from Rostam' (poetic z-). دل نامور is the noun phrase 'the famed heart' (epithet following the noun), and گشت خوش is the compound predicate 'became happy' (گشت = became; خوش = glad). Word order reflects classical/poetic inversion: cause (ز رستم) precedes the subject and predicate.
segments.s435-g40: نزد نیز بر دل is a locative/adverbial cluster 'also near/on the heart' (نزد = near/in the presence of; نیز = also; بر دل = upon the heart). ز تیمار تش is an ablative/causal phrase 'from care, (a) fever' where تیمار = care/solicitude and تش = fever/ardor; together they express a metaphorical burning anxiety 'a fever of care'. The word order places the adjuncts and causes after the main clause as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s435-g1: همی بود is a habitual/past continuous construction: همی (a particle indicating continuous or habitual action) + بود (past of بودن) yields 'used to be' or 'was often'. The sequence places the verb before the subject (poetic inversion): 'بهمن' is the subject and 'به زابلستان' is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/at Zabulistan') introduced by به.
segments.s435-g2: به + noun here introduces the role or circumstance ('as/while' hunting) with نخچیر‌گر as an agentive noun 'hunter/poacher'. با introduces a comitative phrase 'with' followed by a pair می‌و گلستان describing accompanying pleasures — 'fruit (figuratively: pleasures) and rose-gardens'. The whole phrase specifies Bahman’s activity and surroundings.
segments.s435-g3: This is a noun-list of skills/areas: 'سواری' ('riding/horsemanship'), 'می‌خوردن' (a verbal noun 'eating/table-manners'), and 'بارگاه' ('the court' i.e. courtly ways). The items are coordinated with و and presented as the direct object(s) of the verb that follows.
segments.s435-g4: بیاموخت is the past verb (here functioning as a transitive 'taught' in context), placed before the subject رستم (poetic word order). بدان پور شاه is the indirect object: بـ (prefix/ preposition) + دان (here part of the demonstrative/old pronominal element forming 'بدان' = 'to that') + پورِ شاه 'the son of the king' — i.e. 'to that royal son'.
segments.s435-g5: به هر چیز پیش از functions as a comparative phrase 'before/above every thing' (i.e. 'more than anything else'). The clause closes with داشتش: داشت (past 'had/held') + ـش (3rd-person object clitic 'him'), meaning 'he cherished/valued him'.
segments.s435-g6: شب و روز is an adverbial 'night and day' (constantly). خندان is a circumstantial adjective/participle 'smiling' that modifies the subject’s manner, به بر is a locative/goal phrase 'onto the arm' (i.e. 'in his arms'), and داشتش again is 'he held him' — together: 'day and night, smiling, he held him in his arms.'
segments.s435-g7: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. گفتار و کردار are coordinated nouns 'speech and action', and پیوسته شد is a copular phrase 'became joined/consistent' (پیوسته = 'connected/consistent', شد = 'became'). The clause means 'when his words and deeds came to agree.'
segments.s435-g8: در کین = 'in/within hatred' (i.e. 'out of/into enmity'); به گشتاسپ 'toward Gushtasp' marks the target of that hatred; بر بسته شد is a poetic passive/idiomatic sequence meaning 'was bound/fixed upon' (بسته = 'bound/tied', شد = 'became/was'), so the whole means 'hatred became fixed upon Gushtasp.'
segments.s435-g25: The noun phrase 'هنرهای شاهانش' (literally 'the arts/skills of his kings') precedes the verb, a common poetic ordering. آموختم is the 1st-person singular past (classical past) 'I learned' (آموخت + -م). شاهانش carries the possessive suffix -ش ('his'), so the whole phrase is the direct object of آموختم.
segments.s435-g26: 'از' is the preposition 'from' introducing the source. The string 'اندرز فام خرد' omits ezāfe(s) for metrical/poetic effect and should be read as اندرزِ فامِ خرد ('the counsel's hue of wisdom' / 'the tincture/trace of wisdom in counsel'). توختم is an archaic 1st-person past-form verb (poetic/older variant) meaning roughly 'I took/assumed'; here it links back to the same subject 'I' and parallels آموختم.
segments.s435-g27: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (classical usage). پیمان کند literally 'makes a covenant/pact' with کند as the present/habitual or general present 3rd-person singular of کردن. شاه is the subject 'king' and پوزش‌پذیر ('pūzesh-pazir') is an adjective 'forgiving/excusable' modifying شاه; the poet places adjective after the noun in inverted order.
segments.s435-g28: کزین پس is a compound temporal phrase 'from this (time) onward' / 'thereafter' (کزین = 'from this', پس = 'after/then'). نیندیشد is the negative 3rd-person singular present/optative form (ن‑ + بیندیشد) 'he does not/will not think'. از کار تیر is a noun phrase 'of the work/affair of the arrow' (کارِ تیر) where ezafe is omitted; here it idiomatically means 'matters of the arrow' → 'war/armed action'.
segments.s435-g29: نهان من and جان من are two coordinated noun phrases each bearing the possessive pronoun من 'my' after the noun (a common poetic placement). پیش اوست is the predicate 'are before him / lie in his presence' with ست as the classical copula (here 3rd-person singular); the verb is used with the coordinated nouns as a unit.
segments.s435-g30: This is a conditional/alternative clause introduced by اگر ... و‌گر 'if ... or if / whether ... or'. گنج و تاجست should be read with a understood copula (تاج است = 'and a crown it is'); تاجست is the fused form 'تاج است'. The second alternative 'مغز و پوست' lists 'brain and skin' as another possible content; ezāfe and repeated copulas are omitted for brevity, a normal poetic device.
segments.s435-g31: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آن نامه شد is 'that letter became/reached/happened' with شد used as an intransitive auxiliary/'came to be'. نزد شاه جهان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of the king of the world' (نزد = 'near/in the presence of'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect: temporal clause + verb + prepositional location.
segments.s435-g32: پراگنده شد is a passive/stative construction 'was dispersed/was spread'. آن میان مهان is the noun phrase 'that midst/company of guests' (میان = 'midst/among', مهان = 'guests/visitors'); the copular/auxiliary شد follows the adjective/participle to form the verbal phrase. The whole means the guests were scattered.
segments.s435-g57: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing the clause. تا is the temporal conjunction 'until/when' that links to the following verb برآمد (past 'arose/appeared'). برین گاه is a frozen classical phrase: برین = بر + ین (an older demonstrative/marker 'that/this') modifying گاه 'time/occasion' (so 'that high moment'), and چند is a postpositive modifier 'a few / for some time' specifying duration; together the phrase means something like 'when that high moment had come after a little while.'
segments.s435-g58: ببد is an archaic past-form meaning 'was / became' (here 'rose/became elevated'). شاهزاده is the noun 'prince' (the subject). به + بالا بلند is a prepositional/result phrase 'to/up on high' (بالا 'up/high' and بلند 'lofty/high'), indicating the direction or state the prince attained; note classical word order places this phrase before or beside the verb.
segments.s435-g59: This is a coordinated noun/adjective series describing the person: خردمند 'wise' (adjective), و linking to بادانش which is the compound/archaic form meaning 'with knowledge' or 'learned' (here an adjectival/epithet), and دستگاه 'establishment/retinue/court' (a noun indicating his household or apparatus). The three items are joined by و and act as descriptive attributes of the subject.
segments.s435-g60: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'to/into kingship' expressing the goal/result. برافراخت is the past transitive verb 'raised/hoisted'; in classical Persian the object or result phrase can appear before the verb. فرخ کلاه is the object 'the auspicious/fortunate cap' (فرخ adjective + کلاه noun), i.e. the crown. The whole clause means he raised the fortunate cap up to the rank/status of kingship.
segments.s435-g61: بدانست is the past verb 'knew' (here placed before the proper name in a verb–subject order common in older texts). جاماسپ is the subject/name. آن نیک و بد is the direct object: آن 'that/the' qualifies نیک و بد 'the good and the bad' — i.e. the favourable and unfavourable outcomes Jamasp understood.
segments.s435-g62: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'. آن پادشاهی 'that kingship' is the subject of the subordinate clause. به بهمن is a prepositional phrase 'to/for Bahman' (here Bahman is a personal name). رسد is the present/subjunctive form of 'رسیدن' used in narrative to mean 'will reach / come to / be attained by' — so the clause states that the kingship will come to Bahman.
segments.s435-g63: به گشتاسپ گفت is 'he said to Gushtasp' with به marking the indirect object (the person spoken to) and گفت 'said' as the past verb. The following ای پسندیده شاه is a vocative address 'O chosen/beloved king' used to directly address Gushtasp; ای is the interjection 'O', پسندیده 'beloved/chosen' is an epithet, and شاه is 'king.'
segments.s435-g64: ترا is the enclitic object pronoun 'to you / you' (ت = 2nd-person clitic, را the object/topic marker). کرد is the light verb 'made/did' used here in a periphrastic/modal idiom with باید 'must/it is necessary' (باید) to express obligation; effectively 'it was made necessary for you' = 'you must'. به بهمن (prepositional) marks the target 'toward Bahman', and نگاه is the noun 'gaze / regard' used as a verbal noun meaning 'to look/attend to'; together the phrase means 'you must look to Bahman / set your regard on Bahman.'
segments.s435-g49: A prepositional/relative phrase: ز + noun phrase describes origin or circumstance. ز گردون گردان = “from the revolving heavens” (گردان is an adjectival/participle sense ‘revolving’ modifying گردون). که یارد گذشت is a relative clause “which the companion passed,” with که introducing the clause and گذشت as the past verb “passed.” The whole segment sets the scene/circumstance for the main clause that follows.
segments.s435-g50: Main clause: خردمند (the wise man) is the subject. گرد گذشته is a small verbal/adverbial phrase using گرد (around/about/turn) + گذشته (past/ gone) describing motion or a completed circuit, and نگشت is the negative past of گشت ‘to turn/return’ meaning “did not turn/return/alter course.” Together: ‘the wise man, having gone round, did not turn (aside).’
segments.s435-g51: A statement about the addressee. تو is the personal pronoun. آنی here has a demonstrative/pointing sense (“that/just so” / ‘as you were’), and که introduces the relative clause بودی ‘you were’ (past). وزان is a conjunctive/adverbial ‘and/from that’ linking to the following predication; بهتری is the comparative/adjectival ‘are better’ (the trailing ی here marks existence/copying of the adjective into a predicate). The whole segment says: “You are as you were, and thereby you are better.”
segments.s435-g52: A prepositional/location phrase specifying the domain of superiority: به هند و به قنوج = ‘in/at India and in Kannauj.’ بر مهتری uses بر + مهتری where بر marks superiority/’over’ and مهتری is ‘preeminence’ or eminence; together ‘preeminent/superior.’
segments.s435-g53: An imperative/advice construction introduced by ز بیشی ‘from (your) surplus/abundance’ (ز = from; بیشی = surplus/excess). هرآنچت ‘whatever (of yours)’ is the object phrase. بباید is a modal/auxiliary ‘it is proper/ought’ and بخواه is the imperative ‘ask (for).’ Together: ‘From your surplus, ask whatever you ought/desire.’
segments.s435-g54: A simple list of objects introduced by repeated prepositional ز (= ‘from’): ز تخت و ز مهر و ز تیغ و کلاه = ‘from throne and from favour and from sword and helmet.’ Each item names things to be requested; مهر here means ‘favour/kindness’ and کلاه in this martial/royal context functions as ‘helmet/war‑cap.’
segments.s435-g55: Simple verbal clause: فرستاده ‘the messenger’ (subject) + پاسخ بیاورد. بیاورد is a narrative/past form here rendered ‘brought’ (classical Persian present-subjunctive forms are often used narratively as past in poetry). زود is an adverb meaning ‘quickly/soon’ modifying بیاورد.
segments.s435-g56: بدان سان = a set phrase ‘in that manner/so,’ here formed from the prefix ب- + دان + سان; که introduces the relative clause. رستمش = رستم + the enclitic pronoun (-ش = him/it) ‘his Rostam’ = ‘Rostam (him).’ فرموده بود is the pluperfect/past perfect ‘had commanded/said’ (فرموده = commanded/said in an honorific or authoritative sense + بود as auxiliary past). The whole segment: ‘in that way which Rostam had commanded him.’
segments.s435-g121: بماناد is an archaic/poetic optative form meaning “may (it) remain” (3rd person). تا here means “until”/“to (the point of)” and introduces the extent/state that follows. جاودان is an adjective “eternal” modifying the following noun; بهمنم is the proper name Bahman plus the 1st‑person possessive suffix -م (“my Bahman”). The whole clause reads literally “May my Bahman remain until/eternal.”
segments.s435-g122: چو = “when” (temporal conjunction). گم شد is a past construction: گم (lost) + شد (became/was), so “became lost”/“was lost.” سرافراز and رویین are pre‑noun adjectives (Persian places adjectives before the noun): سرافراز “proud/triumphant,” رویین “iron‑clad/armoured,” and تنم is “my body” (تن + -م). Grammatically the clause says “when my proud, iron‑clad body became lost.”
segments.s435-g123: سرآمد here means the “culmination/consummation” or “conclusion” and heads the nominal phrase. همه کار = “all (the) deeds/works,” with همه qualifying کار. اسفندیار is the proper name; the phrase as a whole means “the culmination of all deeds is Esfandiyar” / “Esfandiyar crowned all deeds.”
segments.s435-g124: که introduces the following optative clause (“that/may …”). جاوید بادا is an archaic optative formula: جاوید “eternal” + بادا “may it be,” i.e. “may it be eternal.” سر شهریار = “the chief/head (i.e. sovereign/king)” (سر as ‘chief/leader’ modifying شهریار). Together the clause prays that the sovereign endure forever.
segments.s435-g125: همیشه is an adverb “always.” دل is the noun “heart” (here the subject/experiencer). از + رنج indicates cause/instrument: “from/of suffering.” پرداخته is a perfect/passive participial/resultative form (from پرداختن) used predicatively: “has been expended/consumed” or “worn/spent.” The clause means “his heart has always been worn/exhausted by suffering.”
segments.s435-g126: زمانه is a noun here meaning “the age / the world / fortune.” به فرمان او is an agent/instrument phrase with به used to mark the means/agency (“by his command”), او = “he,” and ساخته is a past participle “made” / “fashioned.” Syntactically this is a separate clause: “the world/fortune has been made at his command.”
segments.s435-g127: دلش = دل + possessive suffix (his heart). باد is the optative particle “may (it be)” placed after the possessed noun in archaic/poetic style (دلش باد شادان = “may his heart be joyful”). The coordinate phrase و تاجش بلند repeats the wish elliptically (باد is omitted but understood): “and may his crown be high.”
segments.s435-g128: به گردن = a locative phrase “on/about the neck.” بداندیش is the modifier “ill‑wisher / malicious (person).” او را is the clitic pronoun with the object marker را emphasizing that he (the hero) is the one affected. کمند is “snare/lasso.” Word order places the locative/modifier before the noun; the clause expresses that an ill‑wisher’s snare lies around his neck (i.e. he is ensnared).
segments.s435-g17: سپردم بدو کشور و گنج خویش is a compact clause: سپردم is the 1st‑person past verb 'I entrusted/handed over' (سپرد#سپر). بدو (به + او) is an indirect object 'to him/unto him' placed after the verb, then the direct objects کشور و گنج خویش 'my kingdom and my treasure' follow; خوی + ش forms a possessive phrase ('خوی' + the enclitic ش = 'his/one's own'), so the whole says I handed over my realm and my treasure to him.
segments.s435-g18: گزیدم ز هرگونه‌ای رنج خویش: گزیدم is 'I chose' (past tense). ز (poetic for از) introduces the source/target of the choosing 'from' or 'to be rid of', هرگونه‌ای 'of every kind' modifies رنج 'suffering', and again خوی + ش marks the suffering as his/one's own — the clause means 'I chose to be free of every kind of my suffering.'
segments.s435-g19: زمانش چنین بود نگشاد چهر is built around زمانش 'his time' (زمان + ـش = 'his time/period'); چنین بود is 'was such' (چنین + بود, past copula). نگشاد is a poetic negative form (ن + گشاد) meaning 'did not open/brighten' (here of the face), and چهر 'face/appearance' is the undergoer; word order is typical poetic inversion where the clause 'was such' licences the following descriptive result clause.
segments.s435-g20: مرا دل پر از درد و سر پر ز مهر uses an older possessive/experiencer construction: مرا دل literally 'to me a heart' (= 'my heart') — مرا (object/possessive role) marks whose heart it is; the copula is omitted, so دل پر از درد reads 'the heart (is) full of pain'. The coordinate سر پر ز مهر is parallel 'the head (is) full of love' (پر 'full of' + ز/از 'of/from' + noun).
segments.s435-g21: بدین گونه بد گردش آسمان: بدین گونه is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in this way / thus' (بدین = به + دین/این in poetry; گونه = 'manner'). بد modifies گردش 'an ill/evil turning' and آسمان is the genitival/specifying noun 'of the heavens' (i.e. fate/heaven); together 'by such an ill-turning of the heavens'.
segments.s435-g22: بسنده نباشد کسی با زمان: بسنده 'sufficient/enough' is predicative; نباشد is the negative present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to mean 'is not / cannot be' (classical usage where the negative form expresses impossibility), کسی 'anyone' is the subject, and با زمان 'with Time' expresses the adversary/thing in question; together: 'no one is sufficient (i.e. can stand) against Time.'
segments.s435-g23: کنون این جهانجوی نزد منست places the time adverb کنون 'now' at the front, then the noun phrase این جهانجوی 'this world-seeker/ambitious man' as subject. نزد + (me) gives 'near/at (my) presence'; the clause ends with an archaic/polite copular form (منست) indicating 'is (with) me / is present' — the copula is older/poetic but functions like 'is'.
segments.s435-g24: که فرخ نژاد اورمزد منست is a relative/descriptive clause introduced by که 'who/that'; فرخ نژاد 'fortunate-born' is a compound epithet describing good lineage, اورمزد (Ormazd/Ahura Mazda) names the god invoked, and منست here functions poetically to assert the quality or favour (i.e. 'is favored/related to'). The whole clause qualifies the man: 'who is of fortunate birth and favored by Ormazd.'
segments.s435-g9: یکی here is the indefinite 'one / a certain' introducing the action; نامه بنوشت is an older poetic past construction meaning 'wrote a letter' (بنوشت = نوشت). به + درد (
segments.s435-g10: همه کار is a noun phrase 'all matters/affairs'; فرزند او 'his son' follows as the recipient/possessor (possessive order is noun + pronoun). یاد کرد is a compound where یاد (remembrance/mention) plus کرد (did) yields 'he mentioned / reminded'. The verb کرد is simple past used here to report Rostam's action.
segments.s435-g11: سر نامه کرد is an idiomatic classical phrasing: سر ('head/beginning') + نامه ('letter') + کرد ('made/did') = 'he began/prefaced the letter'. آفرین here means 'praise' or 'acclamation', and از نخست ('from the first/at the outset') qualifies when he put that praise.
segments.s435-g12: بدانکس 'to that person' introduces the addressee (بد + آنکس fused). که is the relative pronoun 'who/that'. کینه نبودش نجست is a compact, somewhat archaic expression meaning 'harbored no grudge'—نبودش 'he did not have it' and نجست (here) functions as 'sought / took up' in the negative sense 'did not take up hatred'.
segments.s435-g13: دگر 'then/again' signals a new utterance. گفت here is a narrative marker 'said' (verb in past). یزدان گوای منست is a short clause: یزدان 'God', گوای (گویا/گوئی) 'utterance/word' and منست is an older/compressed form meaning 'is mine' or 'belongs to me' (archaic/polite copula). The whole is reported speech: 'God's word is my voice.'
segments.s435-g14: پشوتن is the proper name (Peshotan). ب + دین = به دین ('in/for this faith') where the short form 'ب' functions as the preposition 'to/in'. رهنمای منست = 'is my guide' (رهنمای = guide; منست is the same archaic copula/possessive sense as above). Together: 'Peshotan is my guide in this faith.'
segments.s435-g15: که 'that' introduces the reported content/causal clause. من 'I' is the subject. چند here functions adverbially 'often/many times' (classical use). گفتم is the past 'I said/spoke', and به اسفندیار 'to Esfandiyar' marks the addressee with the preposition به.
segments.s435-g16: مگر introduces a purposive/optative nuance here ('perhaps/so that'—classical use differing from the more common 'except'), followed by کم کند (کم = less, کند = subjunctive/optative 'may do') to express hope/wish that 'he might reduce' کینه و کارزار 'hatred and warfare.' The verb کند is present-subjunctive in sense (optative) inside reported speech.

sentences.s435-s37: The words of King Gushtasp were pleasing; he summoned the auspicious Jamasp.
sentences.s435-s38: That you write a letter to someone near him — to a certain rebellious, revenge-seeking man.
sentences.s435-s39: May God be praised, O world‑champion; we are joyful and light‑souled because of you.
sentences.s435-s40: A scion who is dearer than life; in knowledge he is more famed than Jamasp.
sentences.s435-s21: At the same time he wrote a reply letter, and he planted a tree in a large garden.
sentences.s435-s22: He said thus: 'From the injustice of the lofty Wheel/Heaven, when will harm reach anyone?'
sentences.s435-s23: How can anyone restrain him by forbearance? But if he turns toward learning, that will be enough.
sentences.s435-s24: Peshotan said: 'Whatever you requested, you have adorned my heart with goodness.'
sentences.s435-s57: He tested him repeatedly for a time — at banquets, in battle, and in the hunting-grounds.
sentences.s435-s58: On the polo-field, at feasts and in the hunt — it was said he was like Esfandiar.
sentences.s435-s59: Gushtasp was not at all pleased by him; you deceived him by making him eat/drink.
sentences.s435-s60: He kept saying that the sovereign had given him this — 'I was given sorrow for the sake of care.'
sentences.s435-s53: Esfandiyār said to him, “You — enough; there will remain in the world no one except him.”
sentences.s435-s54: He found him bright‑hearted and eager to learn; from that time on Ardashir called him.
sentences.s435-s55: It was said that he was strong and had a grasping hand; wise, learned, and God‑worshipping.
sentences.s435-s56: When he stood on his feet, his fingertips reached above the knees; his fist was larger (extended) than the knee.
sentences.s435-s45: Of the things that were inside the treasure-chamber: bed-clothes and a bluish dagger.
sentences.s435-s46: From a coat-of-mail and from arrows and bows; from Gopala and from the daggers of the Indians.
sentences.s435-s47: From camphor and from musk and from agarwood (oud); also from amber and gems and silver and gold.
sentences.s435-s48: From the high-born and from seamless robes; from nurses and from unweaned (or not-yet-mature) infants.
sentences.s435-s33: Whatever he sought in his father's learning he obtained; it came to pass, and he became (so) with a handsome bearing.
sentences.s435-s34: It will remain long in a foreign city; no one will read your letter face-to-face.
sentences.s435-s35: One must write a letter to Bahman, like a tree in the garden of Paradise.
sentences.s435-s36: For in the world you have no memorial except him — a reliever of Esfandiyar's pain.
sentences.s435-s41: By your good fortune you have learned culture and counsel; if it seems fitting, now send back a place.
sentences.s435-s42: Send one to Bahman; when you read the letter, do not remain in Zabul.
sentences.s435-s43: For we have come to need your presence; arrange the matter and do not cause delay.
sentences.s435-s44: When the scribe read the letter to Rostam, the man receptive to learning rejoiced at that.
sentences.s435-s49: Golden girdles and a glorious golden ornament; two ruby goblets with golden mounts.
sentences.s435-s50: Rostam handed all of it, pure, over to Bahman; the taker put it into his treasury and counted it.
sentences.s435-s51: Tahmān (Tahmten) came two stages along the road; then he sent him near the king.
sentences.s435-s52: When Gushtasp saw the young scion's face, his steed vanished from his tearful sight.
sentences.s435-s17: Peshotan came and spoke eloquently; he recalled all of Rostam's words.
sentences.s435-s18: He spoke of that same lamentation and counsel and of Arvand, telling of his borders and bonds.
sentences.s435-s19: The famed king was pleased by that, and the arrival of the director proved advantageous.
sentences.s435-s20: Because of Rostam the famed heart became glad; moreover, upon the heart there was also a fever of care.
sentences.s435-s1: Bahman used to be in Zabulistan, hunting, amid fruit and rose-gardens.
sentences.s435-s2: Rostam taught that royal son horsemanship, how to eat, and courtly etiquette.
sentences.s435-s3: He held him above all else; day and night, smiling, he bore him in his arms.
sentences.s435-s4: When word and deed became united, enmity was fixed upon Gushtasp.
sentences.s435-s13: I learned the arts of his kings; from counsel I took the hue (a tincture) of wisdom.
sentences.s435-s14: When a forgiving king makes a pact, thereafter he will not think of the work of the arrow (i.e. of war).
sentences.s435-s15: My hidden (things) and my life are before him; whether it be treasure and crown, or brain and skin.
sentences.s435-s16: When that letter reached the king of the world, that company of guests was scattered.
sentences.s435-s29: Thus, after a short while that high moment arose, and the prince rose up on high.
sentences.s435-s30: The prince, wise and learned and of an established household, lifted the auspicious cap to kingship.
sentences.s435-s31: Jamasp knew the good and the bad that would befall that kingship when it came to Bahman.
sentences.s435-s32: He said to Gushtasp, 'O beloved king, you must set your regard on Bahman.'
sentences.s435-s25: Although the companion passed through the revolving heavens, the wise man did not turn aside.
sentences.s435-s26: You are what you were, and by that you are superior — preeminent in India and in Kannauj.
sentences.s435-s27: From your surplus, ask for whatever you wish—throne and favour, sword and helmet.
sentences.s435-s28: The messenger brought back a reply quickly, in the very manner that Rostam had commanded.
sentences.s435-s61: May my Bahman remain eternal, when my proud, iron-clad body is lost.
sentences.s435-s62: Esfandiyar was the culmination of all deeds; may the sovereign be everlasting.
sentences.s435-s63: His heart has always been spent by suffering; the world has been fashioned by his command.
sentences.s435-s64: May his heart be joyful and his crown lofty; yet an ill‑wisher's snare lies about his neck.
sentences.s435-s9: I entrusted to him my kingdom and my treasure, and I chose to rid myself of every kind of my suffering.
sentences.s435-s10: His time was such that my face did not brighten; my heart was full of pain and my head full of love.
sentences.s435-s11: With the heavens turning so ill in this manner, no one can be sufficient against Time.
sentences.s435-s12: Now this seeker of the world is near me, who is of fortunate birth and favored by Ormazd.
sentences.s435-s5: Rostam wrote a letter and reminded his son of all his affairs.
sentences.s435-s6: He prefaced the letter with praise from the start, to the man who had harbored no hatred.
sentences.s435-s7: Then he said, 'God's word is my voice; Peshotan is my guide in this faith.'
sentences.s435-s8: For I have often spoken to Esfandiyar, that (perhaps) he might lessen hatred and warfare.
